text: The surgical field reveals multiple implants visible on the uterus, left fallopian tube, and left ovary during the abdoperitoneal cytology procedure.

The surgeon begins an unilateral adnexectomy by cutting the fallopian tube and the utero-ovarian ligament. Following the Clermont-Ferrand technique, they open a window below the inframedibulopelvic ligament, cutting the peritoneum between the round ligament and inframedibulopelvic ligament. The peritoneal section is continued to open the retroperitoneal space, where the tumor is located, ensuring clear visualization of the ureter before proceeding with dissection.

The surgeon performs ureterolysis by carefully dissecting and lateralizing the right ureter, which is visible through the posterior leaf of the broad ligament, away from the adjacent ovarian tumor. The connective tissue surrounding the ureter is progressively cut. The infundibulopelvic ligament is controlled and safely cut, followed by the removal of peritoneum and the posterior leaf of the broad ligament, which shows visible vegetation.

The surgeon uses an aspirator to lateralize the ureter, revealing the posterior leaf of the broad ligament. The left hand tracks the ovarian tumor medially, creating a visible space between the ureter and the tumor. A thin bipolar device is used to minimize thermal spread while opening the pararectal fossa, exposing the origin of the uterine artery. The surgeon attempts to circumscribe the large ovarian tumor.

During the dissection, a large pathological obturative node is clearly visible and is removed for pathological examination. The surgeon coagulates the tissue surrounding the node before its removal. The dissection continues anteriorly, enhancing the mobility of the tumor through movements of coagulation, section, traction, and contra-traction. The posterior leaf of the right brown ligament is visible, and the uterine structure is clearly seen.

The surgeon continues ureterolysis, carefully lateralizing and freeing the ureter from the pathological ovarian tumor. Small bites of tissue are coagulated to maintain a dry operative field. The right dissecting plane between the ureter and the ovarian tumor is visible, with connective tissue being progressively dissected.

The surgeon uses traction and contra-traction to free the tumor from its attachment to the right ureter, with the aspirator protecting the ureter laterally. The previously coagulated tissue is cut, and any small bleeders are controlled to maintain a clear view. The residual posterior leaf of the broad ligament is cut, with the ureter safely lateralized two to three centimeters away. The uterine artery is visible, and the remaining part of the tumor is excised without ureteral risk. The adnexectomy is completed, and the tumor is placed in a large amber bag for extraction.

The surgeon performs a medial and infraumbilical laparotomy, closing the amber bag containing the tumor. Hemostasis is ensured, with visible structures including the ureter, the origin of the uterine artery, and ureteral rotation. Bipolar energy is applied to control any small bleeds on the ureter. Multiple biopsies are taken from the uterine serosa, left and right paracolic areas, hepato-diaphragmatic area, and Douglas sponge. A small tumor on the left residual ovary is identified and removed for pathological examination, with bipolar energy applied to ensure ovarian hemostasis.

The surgeon identifies a vegetation on the greater omentum and performs an omental biopsy. Additionally, biopsies are taken from the posterior and cranial parts of the uterosacral area due to suspicious findings related to an ovarian tumor.
